title: Usage
text:
This section explains how to use the Air Analyzer project.


git clone https://github.com/AlessiaRinaldi/air_analyzer.git
cd air_analyzer



Open the firmware project inside the development environment and build it.



Describe here how to flash the microcontroller.



Describe here how the user should connect the device and acquire data.

title: Hardware
text:
This section describes the hardware architecture of the Air Analyzer system.
The device is based on a compact embedded board designed to acquire breathing-related pressure variations inside a sports mask, store the acquired data locally, and allow data retrieval through USB-C.


Hardware Architecture
The hardware system is composed of the following main blocks:
CR2032 Battery
      ↓
Power Management Stage
      ↓
3.3 V Regulated Supply
      ↓
STM32L052 Microcontroller
      ↓
SPI Bus
 ┌───────────────┬───────────────┐
 ↓               ↓
LPS22HBTR        M95256 EEPROM
Pressure Sensor  Memory
      ↓
USB-C Interface for Data Retrieval
The system is designed to be compact, low-power, and suitable for integration into a wearable mask.



Main Components
The electronic system is built around the following components.







Component
Function




STM32L052K8Tx
Ultra-low-power ARM Cortex-M0+ microcontroller


LPS22HBTR
Barometric pressure sensor used to detect breathing-related pressure variations


M95256
256 Kbit SPI EEPROM used for local data storage


TPS61291
Low-power boost converter used to generate a regulated 3.3 V supply


CR2032 battery
Coin cell battery used as the main power source


USB-C connector
Interface used for post-session data retrieval






Microcontroller
The system uses the STM32L052K8Tx, an ultra-low-power microcontroller based on the ARM Cortex-M0+ core.
This microcontroller was selected because it provides:

low-power operating modes;
STOP mode support;
SPI communication peripherals;
native USB Full Speed support;
enough GPIO pins for sensor, memory, and control signals.

The microcontroller manages the complete acquisition and storage workflow. It configures the pressure sensor, handles interrupts, reads pressure samples through SPI, and writes the acquired data to the external EEPROM.



STM32L052 microcontroller schematic


STM32L052 microcontroller with the surrounding hardware required for correct operation.



Pressure Sensor
The selected pressure sensor is the LPS22HBTR from STMicroelectronics.
This sensor is used to measure the small pressure variations generated by breathing inside the mask. These pressure variations are then used to identify inhalation and exhalation cycles.



LPS22HBTR pressure sensor schematic


LPS22HBTR pressure sensor schematic section.
Main characteristics:



Parameter
Value




Sensor type
Barometric pressure sensor


Pressure range
260–1260 hPa


Relative accuracy
±0.1 hPa


Interface
SPI


SPI clock
up to 10 MHz


FIFO
32 levels



The internal FIFO is important for the low-power strategy. Instead of forcing the microcontroller to read every sample immediately, the sensor can accumulate samples and generate an interrupt when the FIFO reaches a threshold.



External Memory
The system uses an M95256 SPI EEPROM for local data storage.



M95256 SPI EEPROM schematic


256 Kbit SPI EEPROM used for local pressure data storage.
The memory size is:
256 Kbit = 32 KB
Each pressure sample is stored using 3 bytes, corresponding to the 24-bit raw pressure output of the sensor.
At a sampling frequency of 8 Hz, the approximate recording duration is:
32,768 bytes / (3 bytes/sample × 8 samples/s) ≈ 1,365 s ≈ 22 min
This local storage approach avoids continuous wireless transmission and allows the user to download the data only after the recording session.



Power Supply
The system is powered by a CR2032 coin cell battery.
A TPS61291 boost converter is used to generate a regulated 3.3 V supply for the electronic components.



Power supply schematic


Power supply schematic section used to provide the regulated 3.3 V rail.
The power supply stage is designed with low-power operation in mind. The objective is to allow the system to operate from a small battery while minimizing current consumption during inactive periods.



USB-C Interface
The USB-C connector is used for post-session communication with a PC.
The system does not continuously stream data during the acquisition phase. Instead, data are stored locally during the recording session and retrieved later through USB.
This design choice reduces power consumption and simplifies the use of the device during physical activity.



USB-C Interface schematic





PCB Design
A custom 50 × 50 mm two-layer PCB was designed for the project.
The PCB integrates:

microcontroller;
pressure sensor;
SPI EEPROM memory;
power supply circuit;
USB-C connector;
battery connection;
test points;
mounting holes for mechanical integration.

 
Front and back view of the custom Air Analyzer PCB.
The compact form factor makes the board suitable for integration into the sports mask.



PCB Design Choices
Some important PCB design choices include:

33 Ω series resistors on SPI lines for signal integrity and EMI damping;
decoupling capacitors placed close to each IC power supply pin;
ground copper pour on both layers to reduce noise;
test points on unused GPIO pins for firmware debugging;
M2 mounting holes for mechanical integration with the mask.




Sampling Strategy
The expected respiratory signal has a relatively low frequency. During intense physical activity, breathing frequency can increase significantly, but it remains well below the selected sampling frequency.
A sampling rate of 8 Hz was chosen because it provides enough margin to capture the breathing waveform while keeping memory usage and power consumption limited.
This sampling rate allows the system to capture not only the breathing frequency, but also the general shape of the pressure waveform.



Hardware Summary



Parameter
Value




Microcontroller
STM32L052K8Tx


Pressure sensor
LPS22HBTR


Memory
M95256 SPI EEPROM


Memory size
256 Kbit / 32 KB


Power supply
CR2032 battery


Voltage rail
3.3 V


PCB size
50 × 50 mm


Data interface
USB-C


Sensor interface
SPI


Sampling frequency
8 Hz


Estimated recording time
about 22 minutes

title: Air Analyzer
text:
Air Analyzer is an embedded system designed to monitor breathing-related activity using a microcontroller-based architecture.
The project focuses on sensor acquisition, firmware development, interrupt-based logic, and documentation of the embedded workflow.

title: Air Analyzer
text:
Air Analyzer is an embedded system designed to monitor breathing-related activity using a microcontroller-based architecture.
The project focuses on sensor acquisition, firmware development, interrupt-based logic, and documentation of the embedded workflow.

title: Overview
text:
Air Analyzer is a low-power wearable system designed for respiratory activity monitoring during physical exercise. The device is integrated into a sports mask and uses an embedded microcontroller-based architecture to acquire pressure-related breathing signals, store data locally, and allow post-session data retrieval through USB.
The main objective of the project is to provide a compact, non-invasive, and energy-efficient solution for monitoring breathing patterns in athletes and during physical activity.



Respiratory activity is an important physiological indicator during exercise. Breathing frequency and breathing patterns can provide useful information about physical effort, fatigue level, and cardiopulmonary response to training.
Many existing respiratory monitoring systems are either too bulky for sport applications, uncomfortable during movement, expensive, or dependent on wireless communication. These limitations reduce their usability in real exercise conditions.
Air Analyzer addresses these issues by combining:

direct pressure sensing inside a sports mask;
local data storage;
low-power firmware management;
USB-based post-session data retrieval;
a compact custom PCB suitable for wearable integration.





The key idea of Air Analyzer is to place a barometric pressure sensor inside a sports mask in order to detect the small pressure variations caused by inhalation and exhalation.
Instead of continuously transmitting data through Bluetooth or Wi-Fi, the system stores the acquired samples locally inside an EEPROM memory. After the recording session, the user can connect the device to a PC through USB-C and retrieve the stored data for offline analysis.
This design choice reduces power consumption and makes the system suitable for battery-powered operation.




The system is based on the following workflow:
Breathing activity
        ↓
Pressure variation inside the mask
        ↓
LPS22HBTR barometric pressure sensor
        ↓
STM32L052 microcontroller
        ↓
SPI EEPROM local memory
        ↓
USB-C data retrieval
        ↓
Offline analysis




The main goals of the project are:

acquire respiratory-related pressure signals during physical activity;
reduce power consumption through an interrupt-driven firmware strategy;
avoid continuous wireless transmission;
store data locally during the recording session;
retrieve data after the session through USB-C;
design a compact PCB suitable for integration into a sports mask;
provide a reproducible hardware and firmware platform.





Air Analyzer is based on three main design choices.


The pressure sensor is placed directly inside the mask. This allows the system to detect breathing-related pressure variations without requiring chest belts, skin-contact electrodes, or external laboratory equipment.



The system stores data locally on an SPI EEPROM. This avoids the need for continuous Bluetooth or Wi-Fi transmission, reducing energy consumption and simplifying the device architecture.



The microcontroller spends most of the time in a low-power mode and wakes up only when needed. The sensor FIFO generates an interrupt when enough samples are available, allowing the firmware to read data in bursts instead of continuously polling the sensor.





The project includes three main parts:

Hardware: custom PCB with microcontroller, pressure sensor, EEPROM memory, power supply, and USB-C interface.
Firmware: interrupt-based embedded software for sensor acquisition, data storage, and communication.
Mechanical integration: sports mask structure designed to host the PCB and position the sensor correctly.





The repository is organized as follows:
air_analyzer/
├── embedded/
├── micro_usb/
├── docs/
├── index.qmd
├── overview.qmd
├── hardware.qmd
├── firmware.qmd
├── usage.qmd
└── README.md
The firmware implementation is mainly located inside the micro_usb/ directory, while the Quarto documentation is generated inside the docs/ folder.

title: Firmware
text:
This section describes the embedded firmware of the Air Analyzer project.


The firmware is located mainly inside:
micro_usb/




Initialize peripherals
Acquire sensor data
Handle USB or serial communication
Process breathing-related signals
Manage interrupts or timers




The main application logic is located in:
micro_usb/Core/Src/main.c
